1: Psalm 72 bears the superscription 'A Psalm of Solomon' — either composed by Solomon or for him. It closes Book II of the Psalter (Ps 42-72) and v. 20 ('the prayers of David, the son of Jesse, are ended') indicates it is the editorial closing of a Davidic collection within Book II. The Psalm is a royal prayer for the king's justice, and its language escalates from possible historical prayer to clearly eschatological vision. 'O God, give the king your justice, and your righteousness to the king's son' — the petition that the king would exercise YHWH's own qualities of justice and righteousness in governance. These are the primary attributes of divine governance (Ps 89:14: 'Righteousness and justice are the foundation of your throne').
2: 'May he judge your people with righteousness and your poor with justice' — the two primary objects of royal justice: 'your people' in general and 'your poor' specifically. In ancient Israelite royal ideology (and throughout the ANE), the king's primary obligation was the protection of the poor, widow, and orphan from exploitation by the powerful. Hammurabi's law code opens with the claim that Marduk commissioned him to ensure the strong do not oppress the weak. Ps 72:2-4 and 12-14 describe the covenant king's essential function: protecting those who cannot protect themselves.
3: 'May the mountains bring peace to the people, and the hills, through righteousness' — the cosmic dimension of righteous governance: right rule by the king produces agricultural fertility and peace in the natural order. The mountains bringing shalom (peace/wholeness) is the covenant blessing of Lev 26:4-6 expressed in royal theology. Just kingship is inseparable from natural abundance — the land itself responds to covenant faithfulness.
4: 'May he defend the cause of the poor of the people, give deliverance to the children of the needy, and crush the oppressor' — the three-fold royal justice mandate: defending, delivering, crushing. 'The children of the needy' (benei evyon, children of the destitute) — the most vulnerable of the vulnerable: the children of those at the absolute bottom of the social scale. These three acts define the king's vocation: not conquest of foreign nations but internal justice, protecting the poor against the powerful.
5: 'May they fear you as long as the sun and moon endure, through all generations' — the eschatological extension of the prayer: not just for the present king's reign but 'through all generations' while sun and moon exist. The sun and moon as measures of temporal duration are the cosmic markers of permanence (cf. Ps 89:36-37: the Davidic throne established 'as long as the sun endures ... like the moon established forever'). The prayer expands from historical kingship to the eternal kingdom that all Davidic kings pointed toward.
6: 'May he be like rain falling on newly mown grass, like showers that water the earth' — the agricultural metaphor of the king's beneficent presence. Rain on newly mown grass was the timing of the spring rains in Israel's agricultural calendar: the early rains after the harvest prepared the ground for the next cycle. The king's just rule is like the seasonal rain that makes agricultural life possible — without it, everything withers; with it, all grows. The metaphor identifies the king's justice with the natural order of fertility.
7: 'In his days may the righteous flourish, and peace abound, until the moon is no more' — 'the righteous will flourish' — the correspondence between righteous governance and the flourishing of the righteous: the just king creates conditions under which covenant-faithful people can thrive. 'Until the moon is no more' — beyond the limits of the cosmic order itself, reaching eschatological finality. The prayer's temporal horizon has expanded to encompass the end of the present cosmos.
8: 'May he have dominion from sea to sea, from the River to the ends of the earth' — the universal dominion formula. 'From sea to sea' and 'from the River (Euphrates) to the ends of the earth' is the maximum geographical extent possible in the ancient Near Eastern conceptual world. This is the Abrahamic promise of universal blessing (Gen 12:3; 22:17-18) applied to the Davidic king, and it is the same language used in Zech 9:10 for the coming king who would 'speak peace to the nations' — the messianic king on a donkey.
9: 'May desert peoples bow before him, and his enemies lick the dust' — the 'lick the dust' was the extreme submission gesture in ancient Near Eastern protocol: the prostrated vassal's mouth at ground level before the king. ANE royal inscriptions and art regularly depicted conquered peoples in this posture. The 'desert peoples' and enemies bowing evoke universal submission — not just the known nations but even those on the margins of the known world.
10: 'May the kings of Tarshish and the coastlands bring tribute; may the kings of Sheba and Seba offer gifts' — specific named locations representing the known world's extremities. Tarshish was in the far west (likely Spain or Sardinia — the most distant point of the ancient Phoenician trading world; cf. Jonah's attempt to flee there). Sheba was in the far south (Arabia or East Africa — the source of spice and gold; 1 Kgs 10: the Queen of Sheba's visit to Solomon). The four directions of the known world bring tribute to the ideal king.
11: 'Yes, may all kings bow down before him; may all nations serve him' — the universal scope: every political sovereign and every ethnic group. This is the eschatological vision of Ps 2:8 and Isa 49:23 combined. In the NT, this vision is applied to Christ — the King before whom every knee will bow (Phil 2:10-11: 'at the name of Jesus every knee shall bow ... every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord').
12: 'For he delivers the needy when they cry for help, the poor and those who have no one to help them' — the 'for' grounds the universal submission of v. 11 in the king's care for the poor: the nations submit because this king is genuinely just. Unlike other ancient conquerors whose power was built on exploiting the weak, this king's authority rests on protecting the powerless. The king's greatness is measured not by tribute extracted but by the needy delivered.
13: 'He has compassion on the weak and the needy, and saves the lives of the poor' — 'compassion' is the Hebrew term for the tender concern that protects rather than exploits. 'Saves the lives of the poor' — the most direct intervention: preserving the very existence of those who would otherwise perish. In ancient law, the death of a poor person who could not pay debts was not necessarily the state's concern; the ideal king makes it his personal business.
14: 'He redeems their life from oppression and violence, and their blood is precious in his eyes' — 'their blood is precious in his eyes' is the king's counter-logic to oppression: in a world where the powerful treated the poor as disposable, this king values the life of the powerless as costly. The ransom/redemption vocabulary is the same used for YHWH redeeming Israel from Egypt. The king who redeems the poor images YHWH's own redemptive character.
15: 'Long may he live! May gold from Sheba be given to him! May prayer be made for him continually and blessings pronounced on him all day long' — the coronation liturgy of prayer and blessing for the king. 'Gold from Sheba' was the most prestigious gold in the ancient world (cf. Job 28:16; Isa 60:6: Sheba's gold coming to Jerusalem in the eschatological pilgrimage). The subjects' ongoing prayer and blessing for the king is the people's liturgical support of the royal office.
16: 'May grain be abundant throughout the land; may it wave on the mountaintops; may its yield be like Lebanon, and may the city bloom like the grass of the field' — the agricultural vision of the righteous king's reign: grain growing even on the mountaintops (normally too exposed for cultivation), yield as lush as the cedar forests of Lebanon (the most densely vegetated landscape in the ancient Near East). This is the reversal of the Deuteronomic curse (Deut 28:23: 'The sky over your head will be bronze, the ground beneath your feet iron') — the land expressing the covenant blessing of right rule.
17: 'May his name endure forever; may it continue as long as the sun endures! May all peoples find blessing through him; may they call him blessed' — 'his name endure forever' echoes the Davidic covenant promise (2 Sam 7:13: 'I will establish the throne of his kingdom forever'). 'May all peoples find blessing through him' (veyitbarachu vo kol-goyim, all nations shall bless themselves in him) — the Abrahamic blessing formula of Gen 12:3 ('in you all families of the earth shall be blessed') now applied to the Davidic king. The king becomes the conduit of the Abrahamic promise to the nations.
18: 'Blessed be the LORD, the God of Israel, who alone does wondrous things' — the doxology that begins the close of Book II. 'Who alone does wondrous things' — divine exclusivity in miracle-working: only YHWH, not the king, not any human patron, performs the genuinely marvelous acts. The doxology redirects the psalm's accumulated praise from the ideal king to the God who gives the king his authority and character.
19: 'Blessed be his glorious name forever; may the whole earth be filled with his glory! Amen and Amen' — the double 'Amen and Amen' closes Book II (as Ps 41:13's 'Amen and Amen' closed Book I). The prayer that 'the whole earth be filled with his glory' echoes the seraphim's acclamation of Isa 6:3 ('the whole earth is full of his glory') and the prayer of Moses in Num 14:21 ('all the earth shall be filled with the glory of the LORD'). The Psalter's editorial design reaches toward this eschatological hope: the God of Israel glorified throughout all creation.
20: 'The prayers of David, the son of Jesse, are ended' — the editorial colophon closing the Davidic collection within Book II. The statement is editorially significant: many psalms after Ps 72 bear Davidic superscriptions, so 'ended' refers to this collection specifically, not to all Davidic psalms in the Psalter. The colophon makes visible the editorial process behind the Psalter: collections of psalms were assembled and organized by editors who left markers of their work. 'David the son of Jesse' — the full genealogical identification emphasizes the historical particularity of the one from whom the royal tradition flows.
